content:
GTA VI is the biggest game ever made, and it's shipping without a disc. That's not really a gaming story. It's a preview of what's happening to your job.

On November 19, 2026, Grand Theft Auto VI comes out. Rockstar spent over a decade and more than a billion dollars building it. And when you walk into a shop to buy the "physical" edition, you get a box with a download code inside. No disc. Rockstar has confirmed there are no plans to print one, not at launch, not later.

Sit with that for a second. The most anticipated game in history looked at the disc, the thing games came on for thirty years, and decided it wasn't worth making.

They're not being lazy. They're reading the room. On PlayStation last year, around 80% of games were already bought as downloads. The disc was already mostly dead. GTA VI just said the quiet part out loud.

Here's why you should care even if you've never touched a controller.

The disc was never the game

Nobody ever loved a game because of the disc.

You loved it because it was fun. The disc was just the delivery truck. For years a big chunk of the games business went into that truck: pressing plastic, shipping it, paying for shelf space in the store. Expensive, slow, and completely beside the point.

Then Steam and the console stores turned the truck into a download. Delivery went from expensive to nearly free. And the moment that happened, all that effort was exposed as overhead. What was left to compete on? One thing. Whether the game was actually good.

Now hold that thought and look at software.

Writing code is becoming the disc

For most of software's history, turning an idea into working code was the hard, expensive part. It took skill. It took time. That difficulty was the whole job, and it's what you got paid for.

AI just turned it into a download. You describe what you want, and working code shows up in seconds, for almost nothing.

So the same question that landed on Rockstar now lands on you. If the hard part is cheap now, what were people actually paying for?

Not the typing. They were never paying for the typing, the same way nobody was paying for the disc. They were paying for what the code delivers: a problem solved, a product that works, a decision made well. That was always the game. Now it's the only thing left to compete on.

This isn't a prediction. It's already measurable.

Here's where it gets uncomfortable, and where the numbers are real.

GitClear studied 211 million lines of code written between 2020 and 2024. They tracked what developers actually do: writing new code, deleting it, and "moving" it, which is their word for refactoring, the unglamorous work of cleaning up and reusing what you already have.

The finding: in 2024, for the first time ever, copy-pasted code beat refactored code. Refactoring fell from about a quarter of all changes in 2021 to under 10%. Duplicate blocks of code jumped roughly eightfold.

In plain English, when producing code got cheap, people stopped tidying up and started pasting. Why merge two similar things into one clean piece when you can just generate a third copy? It runs. It ships. And it quietly rots the codebase from the inside.

That's the trap. Cheap production doesn't make you a better developer. Left alone, it buries you in shallow, duplicated work that someone has to untangle later. The AI will happily write copy number ten. It will never notice you already have nine.

So what are you actually paid for?

The same thing games are paid for now. Being good.

Not the syntax. The judgment underneath it. What should we build, and what shouldn't we? Who is this for? What problem are they really trying to solve, as opposed to what they typed into the ticket? Will this hold up when real people use it? Does the whole thing hang together, or is it nine copies in a trench coat?

That layer, the thinking and the taste and the understanding of the person on the other end, is the part AI can't hand you. It's also the part that was always worth the money. The code was just how it got delivered.

The disc was never the game

GTA VI is telling you something, and it isn't about plastic.

When making and shipping the thing becomes free, the value doesn't vanish. It moves to where it always quietly lived. For games, that was never the disc. It was the fun. For you, it was never the code. It was knowing what to build, and why.

The disc is dead. Good. It was never the game.

And the typing was never the job.
